title: Framing Messages to Deal With the COVID-19 Crisis: The Role of Loss/Gain Frames and Content
population: Convenience sample 18-60yr Colombian Spanish speakers from university mailing lists + Facebook
operationalization_verbatim: 2 Frame (gain/loss) × 2 Content (health/economy) within-subjects design with 8 message pairs presented sequentially. Gain-framed messages emphasized positive outcomes of behaviors (hand washing physical distancing staying home); loss-framed messages emphasized negative consequences of not adopting behaviors. Health content versus economic content.
rob_notes: Adequate randomization to condition; blinding not reported; attrition not reported; low selective-reporting risk due to OSF pre-registration with public materials. Singular-fit models analyzed as GLMs omitting random effects. Manipulation checks showed >91% recognition.

rob_notes: Randomization not clearly described; no blinding mentioned; attrition noted (194 of 270 participated; ~28% attrition); small sample acknowledged

title: Influence of the Framing Effect Anchoring Effect and Knowledge on Consumers Attitude and Purchase Intention of Organic Food

When you decide to purchase organic lettuce you are making a healthy decision'. Negative framing: 'When you decide against purchasing organic lettuce you are making an unhealthy decision'. Low anchor 3 RMB; High anchor 15 RMB (15th and 85th quantiles from pre-survey).
effect_size_value: Negative-vs-positive frame attitude: M=4.461 vs M=3.580 F=61.430; Low-vs-high anchor: M=4.591 vs M=3.446 F=56.728
funding: National Natural Science Foundation of China (71603104
rob_notes: low-to-moderate

title: When How and Why is Loss-Framing More Effective than Gain- and Non-Gain-Framing in the Promotion of Detection Behaviors
population: 229 psychology first-year undergraduates 48M/181F mean age 21.36 (SD=3.07)
operationalization_verbatim: Gain: 'By taking the diagnostic blood test you can find out your current cholesterol level'. Non-gain: 'By not taking the diagnostic blood test you will not find out your current cholesterol level'. Loss: 'By not taking the diagnostic blood test you will ignore your current cholesterol level'.
effect_size_value: Loss-vs-gain/non-gain contrast: beta=0.25 t(222)=4.71; Interaction with Personal Relevance: beta=0.26 t(222)=4.79; Indirect effect through response-efficacy: ab=0.16 95% CI [0.09, 0.25]
rob_notes: Random allocation stated; method not specified; attrition not reported; no pre-registration; relatively small per-cell N

title: Gain-Framed Messaging for Promoting Adult Sport: Examining the Effects of Efficacy-Enhancing Information
population: Adult Canadians 30-69 yr mean 47.0 (SD=8.6); 62.5% female; did not perceive sport as risky (≤5 on 7-pt scale)
operationalization_verbatim: 4-min narrated PowerPoint with 9 consecutive GFM each highlighting a benefit of adult sport (optimal health delayed aging social affiliation fun stress-relief physical-capability thrills competitive-goals travel)
effect_size_value: OE-Travel largest: F(1,219)=12.3 etap²=0.05; no significant behavioral effects

authors: Yu Jiahong; Sun Xixiang; Huang Ying; Jia Yige
title: Concrete or Abstract? The Impact of Green Advertising Appeals and Information Framing on Consumer Responses
journal: Journal of Theoretical and Applied Electronic Commerce Research
n_per_arm: approx 60 per cell across 4 cells (concrete-competence; concrete-warmth; abstract-competence; abstract-warmth)
population: Chinese adult consumers recruited via Credamo online panel; 46.4% female; nationally distributed quota sample
operationalization_verbatim: 2 (message framing: concrete vs. abstract) x 2 (advertising appeal: warmth vs. competence) between-subjects design. Concrete framing employed action-oriented verbs and quantifiable metrics to emphasize immediate tangible outcomes (e.g., 'Join your community to adopt energy-saving products, improving long-term health for families'). Abstract framing utilized adjective-driven narratives centered on values and broader ethical implications (e.g., 'Choose eco-friendly products to ensure a prosperous, harmonious planet for future generations'). Stimulus: new energy vehicle / air conditioner green ad. Participants randomly assigned via block randomization.
funding: This research received no external funding
rob_notes: Adequate randomization via block randomization; attention checks and reverse-coded items used to screen 14 invalid responses out of 253 (5.5% exclusion); blinding not reported; no pre-registration; effect sizes implausibly large (F=1927) raising concerns about manipulation strength and possible demand characteristics; convenience online panel (Credamo) introduces self-selection bias acknowledged by authors. Ethics approval from Wuhan University of Technology.

authors: Remountakis Manolis; Kotis Konstantinos; Kourtzis Babis; Tsekouras George E.
title: Using ChatGPT and Persuasive Technology for Personalized Recommendation Messages in Hotel Upselling
n_per_arm: 175 hotels (Upsell customers) over 2 months for the main CTR/reservation experiment; an earlier pilot ranking experiment ran over 75 hotels for 20 days; guest-level n not reported
population: Hotel guests staying at Upsell-customer hotels exposed to Wi-Fi ad messages
operationalization_verbatim: Pilot field experiment combining ChatGPT-generated ad copy with Cialdini's six persuasion principles (Authority, Commitment, Social Proof/Consensus, Liking, Reciprocity, Scarcity) and Persado's five emotion categories. ChatGPT prompts engineered to generate ad messages of the form: 'Create 3 ad copies about a special offer of -20% (Task) for Couples Massage (Topic), with excitement (Emotion), and funny (Tone), Use an emoticon, in 15 words (Length), in German (Language)'. Emotion categories combined with their top-ranked Cialdini principle (e.g., Joy emotion category paired with Liking). Task: advertise EUR 50 discount on couples massage via Wi-Fi ad served by eXclusivi platform.
funding: PROMOTE (Persuasive Technologies and Artificial Intelligence for Tourism) project (MIS 5093219) of the Operational Program Competitiveness Entrepreneurship and Innovation; co-funded by European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) and Greek Government (Partnership and Cooperation Agreement 2014-2020)
rob_notes: No randomization of guests to conditions described; comparison is between ChatGPT-generated persuasive ads and a single manually written control message rather than randomized arms; no statistical inference (no p-values, no CIs) reported, only descriptive % deltas in CTR and reservation rate by emotion; guest-level sample size not reported; conflict-of-interest risk as authors are affiliated with Upsell S.A. whose commercial platform is the intervention; selective reporting risk high (15 emotions reported but no correction for multiple comparisons); no pre-registration.

authors: Ye WeiMing; Li Qian; Yu Shubin
title: Persuasive Effects of Message Framing and Narrative Format on Promoting COVID-19 Vaccination: A Study on Chinese College Students
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
n_per_arm: approx 74-75 per cell across 4 cells (gain-narrative; gain-non-narrative; loss-narrative; loss-non-narrative); a priori target was at least 50 per cell
population: Chinese college students (M age = 21.44; 46.3% female; 63.7% undergraduate, 36.3% graduate) recruited from Wenjuanxing online panel; participants from 29 of China's provinces; students who had never received a COVID-19 vaccine
operationalization_verbatim: 2 (message framing: gain framing vs. loss framing) x 2 (message presentation: narrative vs. non-narrative) between-subjects web experiment. Health messages were presented as newsletters providing basic information about COVID-19 and the COVID-19 vaccines. Gain-framed messages conveyed the benefits of getting vaccinated; loss-framed messages focused on the potential detriments of not getting vaccinated. Narrative messages presented a personal story from the first-person perspective; in non-narrative messages no specific characters were involved. Example loss-narrative ending: 'don''t take any chances, and get vaccinated in time!'. Participants randomly assigned to one of the four experimental conditions.
funding: The National Social Science Fund of China (Research on Algorithm-mediated Online Platform Labor, grant number 19BXW098)
rob_notes: Random assignment to one of four conditions; a priori sample-size justification (>=50 per cell, Simmons/Nelson/Simonsohn 2011); ethics approval Peking University HSBC Business School (PHBS0401); informed consent obtained; blinding not reported; attrition/exclusions not reported; manipulation checks conducted; not pre-registered; data available only on request from corresponding author (limited transparency). Loss-framed M=3.895 vs gain-framed M=3.563 on 5-point intention scale.

authors: Yoon Ahyoung; Jeong Daeyoung; Chon Jinhyung; Yoon Ji-Hwan
title: A Study of Consumers' Intentions to Participate in Responsible Tourism Using Message Framing and Appeals
n_per_arm: approx 110 per cell across 4 cells (gain-rational; gain-emotional; loss-rational; loss-emotional); plus separate pretest n=140
population: Korean college students recruited via convenience sampling at Kyung Hee University and Korea University after a 140-student pretest of manipulation materials
operationalization_verbatim: 2 (message frame: gain vs. loss) x 2 (advertising appeal: rational vs. emotional) between-subjects factorial design using campaign advertisements as stimulus. Gain-framed loss-framed conditions paired with rational vs. emotional appeals. Example loss-framed copy: 'Your selfishness will bring disaster for travel'. Rational appeals included detailed destination statistics: '(1) The average amount of garbage per day is 3.5 kg; (2) the average water consumption per hotel room is 1.5 tons; (3) more than 70 percent of the travel expenses of tourists go to franchise hotels, restaurants, and large travel agencies, not to the local communities'. Participants randomly assigned to one of four advertisements; involvement in responsible tourism measured as moderator.
funding: National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant No. 2017R1A2B4008866 funded by the Korea government (MEST)
rob_notes: Random assignment to one of four advertisements; pretest with 140 students confirmed manipulation success (gain: t=9.85, p<0.01; loss: t=-17.62, p<0.01); convenience sampling of college students limits generalizability; blinding not reported; attrition not reported; not pre-registered; gain-framed (AA=4.62, IP=4.96) outperformed loss-framed (AA=4.11, IP=4.53) in low-involvement group; interaction with involvement F(1,195)=42.83 to F(1,195)=59.31, p<0.001. Effect size selected: t=4.16 (intention-to-participate gain vs loss).

title: Comparing gain- and loss-framed messages for smoking cessation with sustained-release bupropion: A randomized controlled trial
population: Cigarette smokers aged 18-70 smoking >=10 cigarettes daily with expired CO >10 ppm who had made >=1 prior quit attempt
operationalization_verbatim: Gain-framed: 'If you hold on to your reasons for quitting, you will have a better chance of success'; 'When you quit smoking: You take control of your health. You save your money. You look healthy. You feel healthy.' Loss-framed: 'If you do not hold on to your reasons for quitting, you will have a greater chance of failure'; 'If you continue smoking: You are not taking control of your health. You waste your money. You look unhealthy. You feel unhealthy.' Delivered via two videos and printed materials plus water bottles and air fresheners; all participants received open-label sustained-release bupropion 300 mg/day for 7 weeks.
outcome_measurement: continuous 6-week abstinence from quit date and 7-day point prevalence abstinence; self-report verified by exhaled CO <=10 ppm
funding: NIH grants P50-DA13334, P50-AA15632, K12-DA00167, K05-AA014715, K02-DA16611, R01-CA68427; VA Special Mental Illness Research Education and Clinical Center Fellowship
rob_notes: Randomization in blocks of 6 stratified by gender; allocation sequence created before enrollment by unblinded investigator with sequential assignment by blinded investigator; research assistants blinded to condition; nurse practitioners blinded until end of baseline; attrition ~32% (66.1% completion gain-framed vs 70.4% loss-framed, p=.47); ITT primary analysis non-significant though completers analysis significant

title: Dispositional Motivations and Message Framing: A Test of the Congruency Hypothesis in College Students
population: Undergraduate students at UCLA enrolled in a research methods course (70 enrolled, 7 excluded for missing follow-up data)
operationalization_verbatim: Participants completed BIS/BAS scale measuring approach/avoidance orientation, then received either a gain-framed message ('Great Breath, Healthy Gums Only a Floss Away') emphasizing benefits of flossing or a loss-framed message ('Floss Now and Avoid Bad Breath and Gum Disease') emphasizing dangers of not flossing. Example: gain-framed stated flossing removes particles avoiding bacteria; loss-framed stated if you don't floss particles collect bacteria causing bad breath.
outcome_measurement: number of seven provided flosses used over one week (self-report)
funding: University of California Council on Research Grant
rob_notes: Random assignment to message frame stated; no blinding reported; 10% attrition (7/70); no allocation concealment described; small sample; primary finding is an interaction effect on a single-week self-report behavioral measure
